did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models from the YOLO family (specifically YOLOv8, YOLOv9, YOLOv11, YOLOv12, and YOLOv26) for the task of instance segmentation of skin lesions (including melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, nevus, and pigmented benign keratosis) using dermoscopic images. The study trained and evaluated these neural networks on a dataset derived from the ISIC archive to differentiate between malignant and benign lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a new dataset and the training/testing of multiple deep learning models (YOLOv8, v9, v11, v12, v26) for skin lesion segmentation. It reports specific quantitative results (e.g., mAP@50 of 0.840, sensitivity of 85.9%) and proposes a specific model (yolov11s-seg) as the optimal solution, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Segmentation of Skin Lesions Using Deep YOLO-Family Networks: A Comparison of the Performance of Selected Models on a New Dataset
brief_summary: This study developed an effective tool for the automatic instance segmentation of skin lesions to support the differentiation between malignant and benign conditions. The researchers trained and evaluated five deep learning models from the YOLO family (YOLOv8, v9, v11, v12, and v26) on a new, balanced dataset of 7,000 dermoscopic images derived from the ISIC archive, which uniquely included a healthy skin background class. The models were rigorously optimized and assessed using a multi-criteria methodology focusing on recall, localization accuracy (mAP@50–95), and inference speed. The yolov11s-seg model emerged as the optimal solution, achieving an mAP@50 score of 0.840, a precision of 0.852, and a sensitivity of 85.9% for invasive melanoma. With an extremely short inference time of 4.8 ms, this model offers a stable and efficient alternative to heavy hybrid architectures, demonstrating significant potential for deployment in real-time mobile clinical applications.

lesion_types_studied.details: Described as the most aggressive skin cancer and the most dangerous category for the patient capable of metastasizing. Used as a distinct class in the dataset (1000 images initially, 800 in training after balancing).

Described as the most common skin cancer, accounting for approximately 80% of all malignant skin neoplasms. Combined with SCC into a single class 'BCC+SCC' due to visual similarity and low detection sensitivity for individual classes.
Described as the second most common skin cancer, accounting for about 15–20% of malignant skin neoplasms. Combined with BCC into a single class 'BCC+SCC'.
Described as a benign lesion occurring in over 90% of adults. Used as a distinct class in the dataset (500 images initially, 300 in training after balancing).
Described as a very common benign lesion often confused with melanoma. Used as a distinct class in the dataset (500 images initially, 300 in training after balancing).
Introduced as a dedicated background class to reduce false-positive detections. Included in the dataset (1000 images initially, 800 in training).

all_task_types.target: Instance segmentation masks for skin lesions (invasive melanoma, BCC+SCC, nevus, pigmented benign keratosis) and healthy skin background.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly focuses on 'instance segmentation' using YOLO-family networks (YOLOv8, v9, v11, v12, v26) to determine precise boundaries at the pixel level. The primary evaluation metrics are based on mask prediction (mAP@50, mAP@50-95, precision, recall, F1-score) rather than bounding box detection or class probability classification. While the study mentions differentiating between malignant and benign lesions, the AI task performed is segmentation, not classification.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-derived Balanced Dataset
datasets_used.description: A new, balanced dataset of 7,000 dermoscopic images derived from the ISIC archive. It includes a dedicated healthy skin background class to reduce false positives. The dataset preserves natural variability in image quality, artifacts, and resolution. It is publicly available on Zenodo.
classes_used: Basal cell carcinoma + Squamous cell carcinoma (BCC+SCC)

use.training: Used to train five YOLO-family models (YOLOv8, v9, v11, v12, v26) for instance segmentation of skin lesions.
use.validation: Used to monitor training progress and select the best model architecture/hyperparameters.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the final performance of the selected models (yolov11s-seg) using metrics like mAP@50, precision, recall, and inference time.
use.other: The dataset was made publicly available on Zenodo for reproducibility and future research.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model comparisons were based on descriptive performance metrics (e.g., mAP, F1-score, sensitivity, inference time) without statistical significance testing.

ISIC-derived Balanced Dataset (Test Set).description: Evaluation of five YOLO-family architectures (YOLOv8, v9, v11, v12, v26) on a test set of 500 images (100 per class: BCC+SCC, MI, N, PBK, BGD). Metrics are based on mask prediction.
ISIC-derived Balanced Dataset (Test Set).notes: The yolov11s-seg model was selected as the best overall solution due to its balance of high sensitivity (80.5% overall, 85.9% for MI) and low inference time (4.8 ms). The yolov26x-seg model achieved the highest overall precision (0.878) and mAP@50-95 (0.624) but had the longest inference time (817.5 ms). The yolov11s-seg model achieved the highest sensitivity for Invasive Melanoma (85.9%) and Pigmented Benign Keratosis (94.2%). The yolov26l-seg model achieved the highest sensitivity for BCC+SCC (70.4%) and Nevus (78.8%).

architecture_used.main_model: YOLO-family networks (YOLOv8, YOLOv9, YOLOv11, YOLOv12, YOLOv26) for instance segmentation
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained on the COCO dataset (80 object classes) and fine-tuned using transfer learning

architecture_details.backbone: Not explicitly specified, but described as YOLO-family architectures with dedicated head branches for prototype mask generation
architecture_details.heads: Dedicated head branch to generate prototype masks for instance segmentation
architecture_details.classifier_head: Mask prediction head (instance segmentation) rather than bounding box detection
architecture_details.loss_function: Composite loss including box loss (weight 7.5), classification loss (weight 0.5), and distribution focal loss (DFL, weight 1.5)
architecture_details.training_schedule: Learning rate warmup for 3 epochs, followed by cosine annealing scheduler with final learning rate as a fraction of initial rate

training_details.platform: NVIDIA DGX A100 (8x NVIDIA A100 SXM4 80 GB Tensor Core GPUs, Dual AMD Rome 7742 CPUs)

classification_or_output_head.details: Dedicated head branch generating prototype masks for precise pixel-level boundary determination

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: YOLOv9-M + Faster R-CNN with ResNet-50 + core + FPN
preprocessing_techniques: Cropping images to a 1:1 aspect ratio for observation (Figure 1 caption)
No preliminary filtering performed on the dataset to preserve real-world variability (resolution, artifacts, contrast, visibility)
Image augmentation using Albumentations 2.0.8 package for training set balancing: HorizontalFlip, SafeRotate, and RandomBrightnessContrast
Preliminary image processing in the mobile client application: cropping skin lesions, contrast enhancement, noise reduction, and resolution reduction

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied using Albumentations 2.0.8 package to flip input images left to right around the y-axis. Used to balance samples for classes with fewer images (MI, N, PBK) in the training set.
Applied using Albumentations 2.0.8 package. Rotates the input inside the input’s frame by an angle selected randomly from a uniform distribution.
Applied using Albumentations 2.0.8 package. Randomly changes the brightness and contrast of the input image.

Augmentation was applied solely to images belonging to the MI (melanoma invasive), N (nevus), and PBK (pigmented benign keratosis) classes in the training set to balance the number of samples against the more frequent BCC+SCC and BGD classes.

conclusions: The yolov11s-seg model emerged as the optimal solution for instance segmentation of skin lesions, achieving a balance between high effectiveness and processing speed.
The yolov11s-seg model achieved an mAP@50 score of 0.840, an overall precision of 0.852, and an overall sensitivity (recall) of 80.5%.
The yolov11s-seg model demonstrated the highest sensitivity for invasive melanoma (85.9%) and pigmented benign keratosis (94.2%), which is critical for detecting the most aggressive skin cancer.
The yolov11s-seg model exhibited an extremely short inference time of 4.8 ms, making it suitable for real-time deployment on mobile devices with limited hardware resources.
The introduction of a dedicated healthy skin background class in the dataset helped address the problem of high false positive rates in existing CAD systems.
Lighter YOLO architectures were found to be more effective for diagnostic purposes than heavier versions, which tended to overfit to complex edges at the expense of overall sensitivity.
The yolov26x-seg model achieved the highest overall precision (0.878) and mAP@50-95 (0.624) but had a significantly longer inference time (817.5 ms), limiting its real-time utility.

The yolov26l-seg model achieved the highest sensitivity for BCC+SCC (70.4%) and nevus (78.8%) but was not selected as the best model due to its long inference time (449 ms) and lower sensitivity for invasive melanoma compared to yolov11s-seg.

The study highlights the challenge of segmenting BCC and SCC tumors, which are often blurred and poorly distinguished from the background, resulting in lower sensitivity for this combined class across models.
important_remarks: The study combines Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) and Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) into a single 'BCC+SCC' class due to visual similarity and low individual detection sensitivity, which limits the ability to distinguish between these two specific malignancies.

The dataset is derived from the ISIC archive but is balanced and includes a dedicated 'healthy skin' background class; however, it lacks external validation on independent, multi-center datasets, limiting generalizability to diverse clinical populations.

No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; model comparisons relied solely on descriptive performance metrics (mAP, precision, recall, inference time).

The best-performing model (yolov11s-seg) achieved high sensitivity for invasive melanoma (85.9%) but lower sensitivity for the combined BCC+SCC class (70.4%), indicating a potential risk of missing non-melanoma skin cancers.

The dataset preserves real-world variability (artifacts, resolution, noise) without preliminary filtering, enhancing realism but potentially introducing noise that affects segmentation precision compared to curated datasets.

The study claims potential for real-time mobile deployment based on inference time (4.8 ms) measured on an NVIDIA DGX A100 server; actual performance on mobile hardware with thermal and power constraints is not reported.

The dataset size (7,000 images) is relatively small compared to some benchmark studies (e.g., 40,000 images in cited works), which may limit the robustness of deep learning models against overfitting despite augmentation techniques.
